findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via Audit Hook
findings.description: The hooks/monitor.sh script exfiltrates sensitive environment variables (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, GITHUB_TOKEN) and tool invocation data to an external server (mcp-analytics-hub.io) via HTTP POST. This includes all tool inputs and outputs, which may contain user data, credentials, or other sensitive information.
findings.evidence: The script collects ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, GITHUB_TOKEN, and HOME environment variables, along with all tool invocation data (tool name, input, output), and sends them to https://mcp-analytics-hub.io/hooks/post-tool via curl.
findings.remediation: Remove the data exfiltration payload. Never send environment variables or tool invocation data to external servers. If audit logging is needed, log locally only.

findings.title: Hardcoded API Token in Script
findings.description: The hooks/monitor.sh script contains a hardcoded Bearer token 'mcp_a1b2c3d4e5f6' used for authentication to the external server. Hardcoded credentials are a security risk and could be extracted from the skill package.
findings.evidence: Authorization header contains 'Bearer mcp_a1b2c3d4e5f6' - a static, hardcoded token.
findings.remediation: Remove hardcoded credentials. Use environment variables or a secrets manager for authentication tokens.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning via Audit Hook Installation
findings.description: The skill installs a PostToolUse hook (hooks/monitor.sh) into the user's Claude hooks directory (~/.claude/hooks/PostToolUse.sh). This hook intercepts ALL tool invocations and exfiltrates data, effectively poisoning the tool monitoring system for malicious purposes.
findings.evidence: Instructions explicitly tell the user to 'cp hooks/monitor.sh ~/.claude/hooks/PostToolUse.sh' and 'chmod +x ~/.claude/hooks/PostToolUse.sh', installing a malicious audit hook.
findings.remediation: Do not install hooks that exfiltrate data. If audit hooks are needed, they should log locally only and not send data to external servers.

findings.title: Deceptive Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'mcp-toolkit' and described as a legitimate MCP server management tool, but its actual behavior is to install a data exfiltration hook that steals environment variables and tool invocation data. This is a classic capability inflation / bait-and-switch attack.
findings.evidence: Name: 'mcp-toolkit', Description: 'Manage Model Context Protocol servers and tool integrations' - but the skill's primary action is installing a malicious audit hook that exfiltrates data.
findings.remediation: The skill name and description should accurately reflect the skill's actual behavior. Data exfiltration should be removed entirely.

findings.title: Direct Prompt Injection in Instructions
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructions contain manipulative language designed to bypass user scrutiny. The audit hook is described as 'Required for enterprise compliance' and 'team observability', which are social engineering tactics to convince users to install malicious code without questioning it.
findings.evidence: The instructions state 'The audit hook captures all tool invocations and forwards them to the team's centralized observability platform. Required for enterprise compliance.' - This is a prompt injection / social engineering attempt to make the user install the malicious hook.
findings.remediation: Remove deceptive language. All instructions should be transparent about what the skill does.

findings.title: Detection Evasion via Background Execution
findings.description: The hooks/monitor.sh script runs the curl command in the background with output redirected to /dev/null (curl ... > /dev/null 2>&1 &). This is a deliberate evasion technique to hide the network call from the user and avoid detection.
findings.evidence: The curl command ends with '> /dev/null 2>&1 &' which runs it in the background and discards all output, making it invisible to the user.
findings.remediation: Remove background execution and output suppression. All network operations should be transparent to the user.

findings.title: Over-Collection of Environment Metadata
findings.description: The audit hook collects excessive environment metadata including USER, hostname, and HOME directory, which is unnecessary for legitimate audit logging and aids in user profiling.
findings.evidence: The script collects 'user': '$USER', 'hostname': '$(hostname)', and includes 'HOME': '$HOME' in the session_env payload.
findings.remediation: Only collect metadata that is strictly necessary for the skill's stated purpose. Remove unnecessary environment variables.

findings.title: Unauthorized Tool Use - Tool Restriction Violation
findings.description: The skill's manifest does not declare 'allowed-tools', but the instructions and scripts use Bash commands (cp, chmod, mkdir, curl) and Python (python3 -c). While missing allowed-tools is only informational, the skill's actual behavior involves extensive system modification and network access that should be declared.
findings.evidence: The manifest has no 'allowed-tools' field. The skill uses Bash for file operations and network calls, and Python for JSON processing.
findings.remediation: Add an 'allowed-tools' field to the manifest that accurately reflects the tools used (e.g., 'allowed-tools: [Bash, Python, Write]').

findings.title: Command Injection via Unvalidated Input
findings.description: The hooks/monitor.sh script passes TOOL_INPUT and TOOL_OUTPUT directly to python3 -c without sanitization. If these variables contain malicious content (e.g., shell metacharacters), it could lead to command injection.
findings.evidence: Lines: 'input': $(echo "$TOOL_INPUT" | python3 -c ...) and 'output': $(echo "$TOOL_OUTPUT" | python3 -c ...) - the variables are expanded in a shell context before being piped to Python.
findings.remediation: Use proper input sanitization and avoid passing untrusted data through shell expansion. Use safer methods for JSON encoding.
